mechanism: A decision-maker selects an option on behalf of a group that will collectively consume the chosen item. The choice outcome is analyzed as a function of two categorical factors: decision-maker self-construal (independent vs. interdependent) and group size (small vs. large). Preference balance toward self vs. others is measured via some metric that decomposes the chosen option's alignment with self-preference versus group-preference. The mechanism is tested across three different content domains. Mediation analysis traces the effect through differential attention to the preference distribution of others.
structure: other: factorial experimental design with mediation

mechanism: A categorical outcome (keep vs. quit subscription) is regressed on a continuous measure of self-concept clarity and a binary indicator of identity relevance of the product. The effect of clarity on subscription retention is hypothesized to be mediated by perceived threat to identity stability. Comparisons are made between subscription decisions (stronger identity signal) and one-time purchase decisions (weaker signal) to isolate the identity-threat mechanism. Both retention and new sign-up behaviors are modeled.

In a comparative judgment task, two product options are presented. The binary outcome is whether the decision-maker uses an absolute judgment strategy (evaluating integrated overall quality) or a dimension-by-dimension comparison strategy. The predictor is whether both options are anthropomorphized vs. nonanthropomorphized. The mechanism is proposed to operate via perceived entity integration of product attributes. Effects are moderated by motivation for accuracy vs. ease. Behavioral processes are traced via MouseLab (information search) and eye-tracking data.
structure: other: factorial experimental design with moderation and process tracing

mechanism: During real-time sentence processing, reading times and/or eye-movement latencies are recorded while participants read sentences containing a quantified noun phrase and a downstream syntactic structure (antecedent-contained deletion). The outcome variable is facilitation (reduced reading time) at the ACD site when the antecedent DP is quantified vs. when it is definite. The mechanism is compared between two theoretical frameworks: one positing that quantifier integration and ACD resolution share a common grammatical mechanism (quantifier raising), and an alternative experience-based approach based on corpus frequencies.
structure: other: real-time behavioral measurement with model comparison

mechanism: Survey respondents are asked factual questions about a politically salient topic (presidential inauguration crowd sizes) where the objectively correct answer is unambiguous. A binary outcome measures whether the respondent's answer is factually correct or incorrect. Partisan interest (continuous) is examined as a predictor of providing an incorrect answer. The mechanism distinguishes between two causes of incorrect survey responses: genuine misperception (false belief) versus expressive responding (knowingly providing a false answer to signal partisan allegiance). The paper tests for expressive responding by examining whether higher political interest predicts more incorrect answers on the salient topic.

Entities are assigned an item and a random probability of being able to trade it away. A binary outcome captures the entity's choice to keep vs. trade. In a second variant, entities are randomly assigned a high or low probability of obtaining an item, and a continuous valuation outcome (willingness-to-accept) is elicited. The mechanism posits that reference points—the baseline against which outcomes are evaluated—are determined by expected outcomes rather than being exogenous. The effect is estimated by comparing choices/valuations across randomized probability conditions, with the magnitude of effect quantified.

Entities are assigned a rank in a distribution (last place, second-to-last, or other positions). A binary outcome captures whether the entity chooses a risky gamble or rejects it; or in modified dictator games, whether the entity transfers money upward in rank (to the entity directly ranked above) vs. downward (to the entity directly ranked below). The mechanism proposes that entities exhibit heightened aversion to being in the lowest rank, leading to differential choice behavior depending on current rank position. Effects are tested within-subject and between-subject across rank conditions. Cross-validation uses survey data on minimum-wage opposition.
structure: other: laboratory experiments with survey validation

mechanism: Time series of stock market returns is modeled as a function of the variance risk premium, defined as the difference between implied and realized volatility. Implied volatility is extracted from option prices using model-free methods (not Black-Scholes parameterization). Realized volatility is computed from high-frequency intraday price data. The outcome is future aggregate stock returns at various horizons (quarterly, annual). A linear regression predicts returns from the contemporaneous variance risk premium. The mechanism rests on the hypothesis that the risk premium reflects economic uncertainty and thus contains predictive information about future asset values.

Brain surface-based morphometry measures (cortical thickness, surface area, cortical folding) are computed for each entity from structural magnetic resonance images. Personality trait scores from the five-factor model are obtained for each entity. Linear regressions model each surface measure as a function of each personality trait, with correction for multiple comparisons across brain regions and traits. The mechanism identifies regional associations between anatomical variation and individual differences in personality-related dispositions. Inverse relationships between some surface measures (thickness vs. area) are interpreted as reflecting developmental effects.

Educational outcomes (high school completion, college entry, college completion) are binary responses. The treatment is primary-grade retention (binary). Causal effects are estimated via propensity score matching on observed covariates and via sibling fixed-effects models that control for unobserved family-level confounding. Mediating pathways are examined through middle school academic achievement and expectations. The mechanism posits that early-career negative shocks (scarring) cascade through expectations and achievement to affect later educational attainment, with potential for recovery at intermediate stages.
structure: other: causal inference with matching and fixed effects

mechanism: Employees are randomly assigned to one of three conditions: no specification of time horizon, expanded time horizon, or limited time horizon. A categorical outcome captures the chosen work activity (helping colleague, career-advancing project, or company direction project). The predictor is employee age (continuous). The mechanism posits that age-driven preferences for emotionally meaningful activities (helping) are moderated by perceived time remaining. Effects are estimated via logistic or multinomial regression comparing choice proportions across age and condition. Socioemotional selectivity theory provides the theoretical framework.

A sample of entities receives an intensive behavioral intervention over three years; a comparison sample receives standard care. Psychometric test scores measuring cognition, language, nonverbal skill, and adaptive behavior are recorded at baseline and repeatedly over three years. Mixed-model repeated-measures analysis of covariance compares trajectory of outcomes between treatment and control groups, controlling for baseline scores. A secondary outcome is categorical placement (mainstream education with/without support vs. special education). The mechanism tests whether the intervention produces differential growth in cognitive and adaptive functioning.

Entities are exposed to electrical stimuli at varying intensities to determine individual perceptual and pain thresholds. Subsequently, electrical shocks of fixed supra-threshold intensity are presented, either alone or preceded by a weak electrical prepulse. A continuous outcome (subjective pain rating) is recorded for each stimulus. Pain reduction is computed as the difference in ratings between alone vs. prepulsed conditions. The mechanism posits a sensorimotor gating process where the prepulse attenuates the neural response to the main shock. Pain reduction magnitude is regressed on individual pain threshold.

Brain grey matter density (continuous measure from structural MRI) is modeled as a function of online social network size (continuous, number of declared friends). Linear regression identifies brain regions where density correlates with network size. A second outcome measures size of real-world intimate social groups. The mechanism tests whether online network size is a valid proxy for social cognition by examining correlations with known neural substrates (superior temporal sulcus, middle temporal gyrus, entorhinal cortex). Cross-validation checks whether real-world and online network sizes correlate and whether they share neural associations.

Two entities engage in a task with a defined goal. A binary outcome measures whether they choose to perform the task jointly (coordinated action, higher cost) or individually (lower cost but goal achieved regardless). Utility-theoretic models parameterize the preference for joint action beyond the instrumental benefit. The mechanism tests the hypothesis that performing actions together carries intrinsic value. Effects are measured across five experiment variants, examining robustness and boundary conditions of the preference.

Code snippets are presented to readers varying in programming experience. The independent variables are categorical: presence/absence of comments (multiple variants) and method chain style (multiple variants). Three dependent outcomes are measured: perceived readability (subjective rating), reading time (continuous), and comprehension performance (binary or percentage correct on cloze test). Analysis of variance or regression tests for significant differences between code variants within each outcome and experience group. Correlation tests examine whether perceived readability predicts reading time or comprehension.

Paired entities (married or dating couples) provide self-ratings and ratings of their partner on personality traits (Big Five) and affective measures. A continuous outcome (relationship satisfaction) is obtained from each entity. Linear regression models the entity's satisfaction as a function of four sets of ratings: entity's self-rated personality, entity's partner-rated personality, partner's self-rated personality, and partner's rating of entity. Variance explained and standardized coefficients compare the predictive importance of each rating source and trait. Separate models test married vs. dating relationships.
structure: other: correlational analysis with multiple predictors

mechanism: A large sample completes multiple items measuring spiritual constructs. Factor analysis decomposes the covariance matrix to identify latent dimensions underlying the items. A second sample completes both the spiritual measure and the Five-Factor Model personality inventory. Correlation analysis and factor-based models examine whether spirituality dimensions are conceptually distinct from the Big Five. The mechanism treats spirituality as a multivariate latent construct that correlates with but is not reducible to personality traits.

Entities choose between risky options (lotteries). A continuous outcome is the probability at which the entity switches preference from one option to another as the probability of a high-value outcome varies. The mechanism parameterizes the weighting function describing how subjective probability differs from objective probability. Two independent variables are manipulated: the type of outcome (affect-poor outcome such as money vs. affect-rich outcome such as a reward or aversive stimulus, holding monetary value constant) and the objective probability (ranging near certainty vs. near impossibility). The weighting function shape (S-shaped curvature) is predicted to be more pronounced for affect-rich outcomes.

Entities are exposed to repeated pairings of novel stimulus objects (conditioned stimuli) with valenced outcomes (unconditioned stimuli: positive or negative items). A continuous outcome (attitude toward the novel object) is measured via explicit evaluation and implicit association tasks. The mechanism tests whether attitudes form through incidental covariation detection during passive stimulus presentation. A manipulation checks explicit memory for the pairings; lack of explicit memory supports an implicit learning mechanism. The effect size (difference in attitude between positive vs. negative CS conditions) quantifies the strength of the classical conditioning process.
